content.problemStatement:
Chef has a memory machine. It has one layer for data storage and another layer for cache. Chef has stored an array with length NNN in the first layer; let's denote its elements by A0,A1,…,AN−1A_0, A_1, \ldots, A_{N-1}A0​,A1​,…,AN−1​. Now he wants to load some elements of this array into the cache.

The machine loads the array in blocks with size BBB: A0,A1,…,AB−1A_0, A_1, \ldots, A_{B-1}A0​,A1​,…,AB−1​ form a block, AB,AB+1,…,A2B−1A_B, A_{B+1}, \ldots, A_{2B-1}AB​,AB+1​,…,A2B−1​ form another block, and so on. The last block may contain less than BBB elements of Chef's array. The cache may only contain at most one block at a time. Whenever Chef tries to access an element AiA_iAi​, the machine checks if the block where AiA_iAi​ is located is already in the cache, and if it is not, loads this block into the cache layer, so that it can quickly access any data in it. However, as soon as Chef tries to access any element that is outside the block currently loaded in the cache, the block that was previously loaded into the cache is removed from the cache, since the machine loads a new block containing the element that is being accessed.

Chef has a sequence of elements Ax1,Ax2,…,AxMA_{x_1}, A_{x_2}, \ldots, A_{x_M}Ax1​​,Ax2​​,…,AxM​​ which he wants to access, in this order. Initially, the cache is empty. Chef is wondering how many times the machine will need to load a block into the cache layer. Can you help him calculate this number?
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains three integers N, B and M. The second line contains M space‑separated integers x1, x2, …, xM denoting the indices accessed in order.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing one integer – the number of times the machine loads a block into the cache.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Blocks: indices 0→block0, 3→block1, 4→block1. Loads: first access loads block0, second access loads block1, third access stays in block1. Total loads = 2.
Block numbers: 0,0,1,1,2. Loads occur for block0, then block1, then block2 → 3 loads.
With B=1 each index is its own block: 0,2,0. Every access switches block, so 3 loads.

solution.approach: The array is divided into consecutive blocks of size B (the last block may be smaller). For an index xi its block number is floor(xi / B). While scanning the access sequence we keep the block currently loaded in the cache (initially none). Every time the block number of the next access differs from the current one we must load a new block, increasing the answer by one and updating the current block. The first access always causes a load because the cache starts empty. This yields a linear O(M) algorithm per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(M) per test case, O(total M) over all test cases.

code.Python3:
import sys
def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it)); B = int(next(it)); M = int(next(it))
        loads = 0
        cur = -1
        for _ in range(M):
            x = int(next(it))
            blk = x // B
            if blk != cur:
                loads += 1
                cur = blk
        out_lines.append(str(loads))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))
if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
